nl_question: Which genes participate in the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway?
predicate_note: Original natural-language `participates_in` is not stored in KG2.10.2c; gold uses the reverse-direction `biolink:has_participant` with the BiologicalProcess as subject and the Gene as object, per the q5 predicate_fix in the legacy gold record.

validation.predicate_fix: participates_in -> has_participant (reversed, participates_in not in KG2). Category: Pathway -> BiologicalProcess. CURIE: REACT:R-HSA-191273 -> GO:0006695 (canonical)

verification_guide.summary: 4 canonical cholesterol-biosynthesis genes, chosen to span the pathway from early (HMG-CoA formation) to final (cholesterol formation) steps. Each is annotated to GO:0006695 ("cholesterol biosynthetic process") on its NCBI Gene record under the "Gene Ontology" section, with the strongest available evidence code (IDA, IMP, IC, or TAS — all human-curated experimental or curator-asserted codes). Each is returned by PloverDB for the gold one-hop query.
sources_used: NCBI Gene records, "Gene Ontology" section (provided by GOA, the Gene Ontology Annotation database):
Authoritative GO term page for reference: https://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO:0006695
verification_guide.how_to_verify_an_entry: Open the per-gene URL. Scroll to the H3 section header titled "Gene Ontology — Provided by GOA" (anchor id=gene-ontology). The section has three sub-tables labelled "Function", "Process", and "Component". Each sub-table has exactly 3 columns: [Process / Function / Component] | [Evidence Code] | [Pubs]. Look in the "Process" sub-table for a row whose first column (a wide column) reads exactly "involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process". The MIDDLE column (a narrow column labelled "Evidence Code") contains a 2- or 3-letter code (e.g. IDA, IMP, IC, TAS). Match it against the evidence code cited in each verified_answer. The third column ("Pubs") links to PubMed when an experimental publication exists. NOTE: the Evidence Code column is narrow; you may need to scan carefully — the long-form name (e.g. "Inferred by Curator") is shown only as a tooltip when you hover over the code.

go_evidence_codes_glossary.IDA: Inferred from Direct Assay — strongest experimental code; gene's role in the process was shown by direct biochemical or genetic experiment.
go_evidence_codes_glossary.IMP: Inferred from Mutant Phenotype — strong; a mutation in the gene produces a phenotype consistent with the process.
go_evidence_codes_glossary.IC: Inferred by Curator — a human curator concluded the annotation from related evidence in the literature.
go_evidence_codes_glossary.TAS: Traceable Author Statement — a published statement that the curator can trace to a primary source.
go_evidence_codes_glossary.IBA: Inferred from Biological aspect of Ancestor — phylogenetic propagation from a related gene.
go_evidence_codes_glossary.IEA: Inferred from Electronic Annotation — computational only (we treat as weakest and don't use as the cited evidence).

verification_guide.cross_check_with_ploverdb: Each verified_answer's CURIE (NCBIGene:3156, 3157, 4047, 1717) was confirmed present in the PloverDB result set for the gold one-hop query (n0=biolink:Gene, n1=GO:0006695, e0=biolink:has_participant from process to gene). PloverDB returned 33 gene CURIEs total; the 4 here are pathway-representative early/mid/late enzymes chosen for clarity of evidence.

evidence.publisher: U.S. National Center for Biotechnology Information — NCBI Gene; GO annotation from GOA
evidence.where_on_page: Open the HMGCS1 page, then jump to the "Gene Ontology — Provided by GOA" H3 (anchor #gene-ontology). In the PROCESS sub-table, multiple rows say "involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process". The fastest way to find the right one: SCAN THE RIGHTMOST COLUMN ("Pubs") FOR THE WORD "PubMed" — exactly one of the bare-term rows has it. That row's middle column shows the evidence code "IC" (tooltip: "Inferred by Curator"). A separate row has evidence code "TAS" (Traceable Author Statement) — semantically also valid, but its Pubs column is empty, so IC is the cited evidence here.
evidence.exact_quote: involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process IC PubMed

verified_answers.label: HMGCR
verified_answers.pathway_step: rate-limiting (HMG-CoA → mevalonate); target of statin drugs

evidence.publisher: U.S. National Center for Biotechnology Information — NCBI Gene; GO annotation from GOA
evidence.where_on_page: Open the HMGCR page, then jump to the "Gene Ontology — Provided by GOA" H3 (anchor #gene-ontology). The section has 3 sub-tables: Function, Process, Component — each with exactly 3 columns: [term] | [Evidence Code] | [Pubs]. In the PROCESS sub-table, find the row whose first (wide) column reads exactly "involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process". In the middle (narrow) Evidence Code column the cell reads "IDA" — tooltip "Inferred from Direct Assay" (the strongest experimental GO code). The right column shows "PubMed" linked to the supporting paper.
evidence.exact_quote: involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process IDA PubMed

evidence.page_title: LSS lanosterol synthase [Homo sapiens (human)] — NCBI Gene
evidence.publisher: U.S. National Center for Biotechnology Information — NCBI Gene; GO annotation from GOA
evidence.where_on_page: Open the LSS page, then jump to the "Gene Ontology — Provided by GOA" H3 (anchor #gene-ontology). In the PROCESS sub-table, multiple rows say "involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process" (4 with the bare term + 2 with suffixes "via desmosterol" / "via lathosterol"). The fastest way to find the right one: SCAN THE RIGHTMOST COLUMN ("Pubs") FOR THE WORD "PubMed" — exactly one of these rows has it. That row's middle (Evidence Code) column shows "IMP" (tooltip: "Inferred from Mutant Phenotype"). The other matching rows carry IBA / IEA / TAS codes with empty Pubs columns — those are weaker evidence and not the cited row.
evidence.exact_quote: involved_in cholesterol biosynthetic process IMP PubMed

verified_answers.label: DHCR7
verified_answers.pathway_step: final step (7-dehydrocholesterol → cholesterol; mutations cause Smith-Lemli-Opitz syndrome)
